_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: No one has ever seen his face. That was the whole point — and it worked better than he ever promised.

museum_objects.title: Bitcoin Whitepaper (Original PDF)
museum_objects.description: The nine-page document titled 'Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System,' published by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto, released without copyright restriction.
museum_objects.significance: This is Hiro's birth certificate, except no one signed their real name to it — a stranger handed the world a blueprint and then disappeared, and that vanishing act became the entire foundation of who he is.
museum_objects.license: Released without copyright restriction by Satoshi Nakamoto; public domain/freely distributable
museum_objects.plain_language: The original nine-page document that first described Bitcoin, written by an anonymous person and given away for free with no copyright.
museum_objects.licence: Released without copyright restriction by Satoshi Nakamoto; public domain/freely distributable

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so. | IMAGE QA FAIL: The image does not show the original PDF of the Bitcoin Whitepaper, but rather a printed booklet with the same title.

museum_objects.title: Genesis Block (Block 0)
museum_objects.description: The first-ever block mined on the Bitcoin network, containing a reward address that has never once been spent.
museum_objects.significance: Hiro's address 1A1zP1eP5QGefi2DMPTfTL5SLmv7DivfNa has sat untouched since the day he was born — the one thing in his whole existence that has never moved.
museum_objects.license: Public blockchain data; explainer article copyrighted
museum_objects.plain_language: The very first block of the Bitcoin network, created in January 2009, whose reward money has never been spent to this day.
museum_objects.licence: Public blockchain data; explainer article copyrighted

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so. | IMAGE QA FAIL: The image shows source code, not the Genesis Block itself, which is a data structure or block of data on the Bitcoin network.

museum_objects.title: The Times of London, Jan 3, 2009 (Embedded Headline)
museum_objects.description: The headline 'The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks' was embedded directly into the Genesis Block's code as a timestamp and statement.
museum_objects.significance: Hiro was born quoting the very financial system he was built to replace — his first words, encoded forever, were literally a headline about banks failing.
museum_objects.license: Forum discussion, community sourced; original newspaper copyrighted to The Times
museum_objects.plain_language: A real newspaper headline about a bank bailout that Bitcoin's creator hid inside the very first block of the network, as permanent proof of the exact date it was born.
museum_objects.licence: Forum discussion, community sourced; original newspaper copyrighted to The Times

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: A Cypherpunk's Manifesto (Eric Hughes, 1993)
museum_objects.description: The foundational ideological text of the cypherpunk movement, arguing that privacy in an electronic age requires cryptography, not laws.
museum_objects.significance: Fifteen years before Hiro existed, someone wrote down exactly why he'd need to exist — this manifesto is his ideology's grandfather.
museum_objects.license: Historical text, widely reproduced without restriction
museum_objects.plain_language: A 1993 essay that first argued people need private, uncrackable codes to protect their freedom — the philosophy Bitcoin was later built on.
museum_objects.licence: Historical text, widely reproduced without restriction

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Casascius Physical Bitcoin Coin
museum_objects.material: metal (brass/silver) with embedded holographic private key
museum_objects.description: Physical brass or silver tokens created by Mike Caldwell, each embedded with a real private key holding actual bitcoin, held today in the British Museum's numismatic collection.
museum_objects.significance: For a currency that was supposed to exist only as code, someone eventually needed to hold him in their hand — the British Museum agreed, and put him in a case next to coins two thousand years older.
museum_objects.license: British Museum collection licensing (permission required for commercial/documentary use)
museum_objects.plain_language: A real physical coin, made in the early 2010s, that actually held real bitcoin inside it — now kept in the British Museum next to ancient coins thousands of years old.
museum_objects.licence: British Museum collection licensing (permission required for commercial/documentary use)

resolved.via: TIER 3 — no API, hard 403. Substitute with an open-licence equivalent.

world_map.center_label: London, UK (embedded newspaper reference) / Decentralized origin
world_map.map_description: Hiro has no hometown — he was born from an anonymous whitepaper, referenced a London newspaper in his first block, and spread through a leaderless global network with no headquarters.

nodes.label: Decentralized / Unknown Origin
nodes.description: Satoshi Nakamoto's real identity and location have never been confirmed — Hiro's true birthplace is unknown by design.

nodes.label: London, UK (The Times headline)
nodes.description: The Times of London newspaper headline from January 3, 2009 was embedded permanently into the Genesis Block.

nodes.label: British Museum, London
nodes.description: Home to a real Casascius physical bitcoin coin in its numismatic collection.

nodes.label: Sichuan, China (mining farms)
nodes.description: Site of major bitcoin mining operations before China's 2021 crackdown forced miners to flee overseas.

nodes.label: Global Peer-to-Peer Network
nodes.description: Bitcoin runs on a leaderless, distributed network of computers worldwide with no single physical headquarters.
